title: Lazy
description: The #[Lazy] attribute makes a component load only when it becomes visible in the viewport, preventing slow components from blocking the initial page render.

sections.title: #Basic usage
sections.content:
Apply the #[Lazy] attribute to any component that should be lazy-loaded: With #[Lazy], the component initially renders as an empty
, then loads when it enters the viewport—typically when a user scrolls to it.

examples.code:
amount = Transaction::monthToDate()->sum('amount'); }};?> 
Revenue this month: {{ $amount }}

sections.title: #Lazy vs Defer
sections.content:
Livewire provides two ways to delay component loading:
Use lazy loading for components below the fold that users might not scroll to. Use defer for components that are always visible but you want to load after the page renders.

sections.title: #Rendering placeholders
sections.content:
By default, Livewire renders an empty
before the component loads. You can provide a custom placeholder using the placeholder() method: Users will see a skeleton placeholder until the component enters the viewport and loads. If your placeholder's root element is a
, your component must also use a
element.

examples.code:
amount = Transaction::monthToDate()->sum('amount'); }  public function placeholder() { return <<<'HTML'
HTML; } };?> 
Revenue this month: {{ $amount }}

sections.title: #Bundling requests
sections.content:
By default, lazy components load in parallel with independent network requests. To bundle multiple lazy components into a single request, use the bundle parameter:
Now, if there are ten revenue components on the page, all ten will load via a single bundled network request instead of ten parallel requests.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/⚡revenue.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\Lazy;use Livewire\Component; new #[Lazy(bundle: true)] class extends Component { // ...};

sections.title: #Alternative approach
sections.content:
Instead of the attribute, you can lazy-load specific component instances using the lazy parameter:
This is useful when you only want certain instances of a component to be lazy-loaded.
If a component has #[Lazy] but you want to load it immediately in certain cases, you can override it:

sections.title: #Learn more
sections.content: For complete documentation on lazy loading, including placeholders, bundling strategies, and passing props, see the Lazy Loading documentation.
